name: Battle of Moore's Creek Bridge
strategic_significance: Ended organized Loyalist resistance in NC; stalled British southern strategy for two years; contributed to Halifax Resolves (April 12, 1776 — first colonial resolution explicitly calling for independence); first Patriot battlefield victory of the Revolution
terrain_notes: Dark sluggish creek 35–50ft wide, estimated 6ft deep at bridge. Single bridge on sandbar. Slight rise on east bank with reconstructed earthworks confirmed by NPS archaeology exactly on original position. Swampy approaches both sides. Original road still visible in park.
description: Decisive Patriot victory over the Loyalist Highland Scots column. Patriots under Caswell and Lillington fortified the east bank of Moore's Creek and removed the bridge planks before dawn, smearing the remaining log stringers with soap and animal fat. MacLeod and Campbell led approximately 75 Highland broadswordsmen across the bare greased girders. Patriot fire — muskets and swanshot artillery — opened at 30 paces. MacLeod was killed at the bridge. The Loyalist column collapsed; approximately 850 prisoners and the Highland Scots' military capacity in the Cape Fear region were lost. Reconstruction confidence is Medium: unit positions are original reconstructions from Moore's after-action report (CSR 11-0146) and Rankin (1953 NCHR), corroborated by NPS archaeology. Exact troop dispositions and timing are approximations.
